reasoning: On Friday, August 1, 2025, a weaker-than-expected US jobs report (73K vs 106K expected) shocked the market, causing corporate bond spreads to spike from 0.44 to 0.47. Prior to this, credit spreads had reached 18-year lows (as noted by Goldman Sachs). ICE BofA AA Corporate OAS (BAMLC0A2CAA) hit an absolute floor of 0.43% just days prior. Because we are starting near the historic floor, the risk profile is highly asymmetric—spreads have very little room to compress further but immense room to widen. The short-term reaction to the weak jobs data provides strong upward momentum, meaning spreads are highly likely to be elevated by August 10 and September 2. Over the longer term (November 2025 and January 2026), uncertainty increases (probabilities regress toward 0.5), but mean reversion from historic lows implies spreads will likely remain above the ~0.47 baseline. Therefore, the probability of an increase is highest in the short term and gradually slopes toward 0.5 over longer horizons.
